q: The Mauryan period is known for which architectural innovation?
options: Polished stone pillars (Ashokan pillars with capitals — e.g., Sarnath Lion Capital)
explanation: The Mauryan period (c. 322-185 BCE) is known for polished stone pillars — the Ashokan pillars. Each pillar is a single piece of sandstone, polished to a mirror-like finish, topped with a capital (Sarnath Lion Capital = national emblem). The Barabar Caves (Bihar, rock-cut, donated to Ajivikas) are also Mauryan. The pillars represent the beginning of Indian stone architecture.

q: The Sarnath Lion Capital, which is India's national emblem, was built during which period?
options: The Mauryan period (c. 250 BCE, by Ashoka)
explanation: The Sarnath Lion Capital (four lions standing back to back, with a Dhamma Chakra on top) was built by Ashoka (c. 250 BCE, Mauryan period). It crowns the Sarnath Pillar. Adopted as India's national emblem (26 January 1950). The chakra (wheel) below the lions appears on India's national flag.

q: The Barabar Caves (near Gaya, Bihar) — the oldest rock-cut caves in India — were donated by Ashoka to which sect?
options: The Ajivikas (an ascetic sect founded by Makkhali Gosala)
explanation: The Barabar Caves (near Gaya, Bihar) — the oldest rock-cut caves in India — were donated by Ashoka to the Ajivikas (an ascetic sect founded by Makkhali Gosala, a contemporary of Buddha and Mahavira). The Ajivikas believed in niyati (destiny/fate). Ashoka's grandson Dasharatha also donated caves at nearby Nagarjuna hills.

q: The Stupa architecture (Buddhist hemispherical domes) reached its peak at which site?
options: The Great Stupa at Sanchi (Madhya Pradesh, enlarged during the Shunga period)
explanation: The Great Stupa at Sanchi (Madhya Pradesh) is the finest example of stupa architecture. Originally built by Ashoka (c. 250 BCE), it was enlarged during the Shunga period (c. 2nd c. BCE). It has four toranas (gateways) with magnificent sculptures. UNESCO World Heritage Site (1989). Stupas contain relics of the Buddha or his disciples.

q: The Gandhara School of Art (c. 1st-3rd century CE) is characterized by which feature?
options: Greco-Roman style (Hellenistic features: wavy hair, muscular body, Greek drapery)
explanation: The Gandhara School (c. 1st-3rd c. CE, Kushana period) is Greco-Roman in style — combining Hellenistic (Greek) and Indian elements. Features: wavy hair, muscular body, Greek drapery, sandals, blue-grey schist stone. Located in Gandhara region (Peshawar, Taxila, modern Pakistan/Afghanistan). Produced the first human images of the Buddha.

q: The Mathura School of Art (c. 1st-3rd century CE) is characterized by which feature?
options: Indigenous Indian style (usnisa/topknot, serene expression, transparent robes, red sandstone)
explanation: The Mathura School (c. 1st-3rd c. CE, Kushana period) is indigenous Indian in style. Features: usnisa (topknot), serene spiritual expression, transparent robes, red sandstone. Located at Mathura (UP). Unlike Gandhara (Buddhist only), Mathura produced Buddhist, Jain, and Hindu images. It developed independently of Greco-Roman influence.

q: The Amravati (Amaravati) School of Art flourished in which region?
options: The lower Krishna valley (Andhra Pradesh, c. 2nd century BCE - 3rd century CE)
explanation: The Amaravati School flourished in the lower Krishna valley (Andhra Pradesh, c. 2nd c. BCE - 3rd c. CE) — under the Satavahanas. It is known for white marble sculptures and the Amaravati Stupa (with magnificent limestone reliefs depicting Jataka tales and Buddhist themes). The sculptures are now in the British Museum, Chennai Government Museum, and other collections.

q: The Nagara style of temple architecture is associated with which region?
options: North India (characterized by a curvilinear shikhara over the sanctum)
explanation: The Nagara style is the North Indian temple architecture style. Key feature: a curvilinear shikhara (tower) over the garbhagriha (sanctum). Examples: Dashavatara Temple (Deogarh, UP, Gupta period), Kandariya Mahadeo (Khajuraho), Lingaraj (Bhubaneswar), Sun Temple (Konark). The Nagara style developed from the Gupta period onwards.

q: The Dravida style of temple architecture is associated with which region?
options: South India (characterized by a vimana (pyramidal tower) over the sanctum and a gopuram gateway)
explanation: The Dravida style is the South Indian temple architecture style. Key features: a vimana (pyramidal tower) over the garbhagriha, a gopuram (gateway tower), large enclosure walls, mandapas (halls). Examples: Brihadeshwara Temple (Tanjore, Chola), Meenakshi Temple (Madurai), Kailasanatha (Kanchipuram, Pallava). Developed from the Pallava period onwards.

q: The earliest Hindu temples (flat-roofed, with a small shikhara) were built during which period?
options: The Gupta period (c. 4th-6th century CE, e.g., Dashavatara Temple at Deogarh)
explanation: The earliest Hindu temples were built during the Gupta period (c. 4th-6th c. CE). They started as flat-roofed structures with a small shikhara over the sanctum. The Dashavatara Temple at Deogarh (UP, Gupta period) is a classic early example — dedicated to Vishnu, with a square shikhara. The Gupta temples set the template for all later Indian temple architecture.

q: The Ajanta Caves (Maharashtra) are famous for which type of art?
options: Buddhist paintings (frescoes) depicting Jataka tales and Buddhist themes
explanation: The Ajanta Caves (Maharashtra, 29 caves) are famous for Buddhist paintings (frescoes) — depicting Jataka tales (previous lives of the Buddha) and Buddhist themes. Mostly Gupta period (4th-6th c. CE, caves 16-27) with some earlier (2nd c. BCE). UNESCO (1983). The frescoes are considered the finest ancient Indian art. Chinese pilgrim Hiuen Tsang visited (7th c.).

q: The Ellora Caves (Maharashtra) are unique for which reason?
options: They contain Hindu, Buddhist, AND Jain caves (34 caves, c. 6th-10th century CE)
explanation: The Ellora Caves (Maharashtra, 34 caves, c. 6th-10th c. CE) are unique — they contain Hindu (caves 14-29), Buddhist (caves 1-12), AND Jain (caves 30-34) caves side by side. The Kailasa Temple (cave 16, Hindu, Rashtrakuta king Krishna I, c. 760 CE) is the largest monolithic (single-rock) structure in the world. UNESCO (1983).

q: The Kailasa Temple at Ellora (cave 16) — the largest monolithic structure in the world — was built by which dynasty?
options: The Rashtrakuta dynasty (king Krishna I, c. 760 CE)
explanation: The Kailasa Temple at Ellora (cave 16) was built by the Rashtrakuta king Krishna I (c. 760 CE). It is the largest monolithic (single-rock) structure in the world — carved top-down from a single basalt cliff. Dedicated to Shiva. It represents the peak of Indian rock-cut architecture. Part of the Ellora UNESCO site (1983).

q: Kalidasa's 'Abhijnanasakuntalam' (Shakuntala) is a work in which genre?
explanation: Kalidasa's 'Abhijnanasakuntalam' (Shakuntala) is a Sanskrit drama (play) — considered the greatest Sanskrit play. It tells the story of King Dushyanta and Shakuntala (a forest nymph's daughter). Translated into English by William Jones (1789) — created a sensation in Europe. Goethe admired it. Kalidasa was a Navaratna in Chandragupta II's court (Gupta period).

q: The 'Arthashastra' (treatise on statecraft) was written by which author?
explanation: The 'Arthashastra' was written by Kautilya (Chanakya) — Chandragupta Maurya's advisor (c. 4th century BCE). It covers statecraft, economics, military strategy, law, and administration. It describes the Mauryan administrative system (18 departments/tirthas). Rediscovered in 1909 by R. Shamasastry. It is the most important political treatise of ancient India.

q: Panini's 'Ashtadhyayi' is a foundational text in which field?
options: Sanskrit grammar (8 chapters, 3,959 sutras — the most systematic grammar ever written)
explanation: Panini's 'Ashtadhyayi' (8 chapters, 3,959 sutras) is the most systematic Sanskrit grammar ever written — and one of the greatest works of linguistics in world history. Panini lived c. 5th-4th century BCE (pre-Mauryan, in Gandhara/northwest). His grammar codified Classical Sanskrit. Patanjali's Mahabhashya (Shunga period) is a commentary on it.

q: The 'Charaka Samhita' and 'Sushruta Samhita' are foundational texts in which field?
options: Medicine (Ayurveda — Charaka = internal medicine, Sushruta = surgery)
explanation: The 'Charaka Samhita' (internal medicine) and 'Sushruta Samhita' (surgery) are the foundational texts of Ayurveda (Indian medicine). Charaka (c. 1st-2nd c. CE) systematized internal medicine. Sushruta (c. 6th c. BCE) is the 'father of Indian surgery' — he described 120 surgical instruments, 300 procedures, and plastic surgery (rhinoplasty/nose reconstruction). Both were refined during the Gupta period.

q: Aryabhata's 'Aryabhatiya' (c. 499 CE) is a foundational text in which field?
options: Mathematics and astronomy (pi, Earth rotation, eclipses, sine table)
explanation: Aryabhata's 'Aryabhatiya' (c. 499 CE, Gupta period) is a foundational text in mathematics and astronomy. It covers: calculation of pi (~3.1416), the Earth's rotation on its axis, explanation of lunar and solar eclipses (shadows), place-value system, sine table. Aryabhata was 23 when he wrote it. India's first satellite was named 'Aryabhata' (1975).

q: The 'Silappadikaram' (Tamil epic) was written by which author?
options: Ilango Adigal (a Chera prince, c. 2nd century CE, Sangam period)
explanation: The 'Silappadikaram' (The Anklet Story) was written by Ilango Adigal (a Chera prince, c. 2nd c. CE, Sangam period). It is one of the five great epics of Tamil literature. It tells the story of Kovalan and Kannagi (the ideal wife who burns Madurai). It celebrates the Pattini (chaste wife) cult. It also provides rich information about Sangam Age society and culture.

q: The 'Thirukkural' (1,330 couplets) was written by which author?
explanation: The 'Thirukkural' was written by Thiruvalluvar (c. 1st-2nd c. CE, Sangam period). It has 1,330 couplets (kurals) in 133 chapters, divided into three books: Aram (Virtue/Ethics), Porul (Wealth/Politics), and Inbam (Love). It is considered the 'Tamil Veda' — a universal text on ethics and morality. Translated into many languages worldwide.

q: The Iron Pillar of Delhi (Mehrauli) — famous for not rusting — was built during which period?
options: The Gupta period (c. 4th century CE, during Chandragupta II's reign)
explanation: The Iron Pillar of Delhi (Mehrauli) was built during the Gupta period (c. 4th c. CE, Chandragupta II's reign). It is ~7 meters tall, ~6 tons, and has NOT rusted in ~1600 years — a testament to Gupta-era metallurgy. It has a Sanskrit inscription praising 'Chandra' (Chandragupta II). Originally at Udayagiri, later moved to Delhi.

q: The Brihadeshwara Temple (Tanjore, Tamil Nadu) — a masterpiece of Dravida architecture — was built by which dynasty?
options: The Chola dynasty (Rajaraja I, c. 1010 CE)
explanation: The Brihadeshwara Temple (Rajarajeshvara Temple, Tanjore, TN) was built by the Chola king Rajaraja I (c. 1010 CE). It is a masterpiece of Dravida architecture — with a 66-meter vimana (tower), a massive Nandi bull, and wall paintings. UNESCO World Heritage Site (1987, as 'Great Living Chola Temples'). Dedicated to Shiva. The vimana is topped with a single 80-ton granite stone.

q: Bihar's contribution to ancient Indian art and architecture includes which of the following?
options: All of the above (Ashokan pillars at Lauriya/Rampurva, Barabar Caves, Nalanda University, Mahabodhi Temple)
explanation: Bihar's contributions: (1) Ashokan pillars at Lauriya-Araraj, Lauriya-Nandangarh, Rampurva (West Champaran). (2) Barabar Caves (near Gaya — oldest rock-cut caves in India, donated to Ajivikas). (3) Nalanda University (5th-12th c. CE — Buddhist monasteries, stupas, sculptures). (4) Mahabodhi Temple (Bodh Gaya — original built by Ashoka, rebuilt in Gupta era). Bihar was a centre of Buddhist art and architecture!

q: The 'Mahabodhi Temple' at Bodh Gaya (Bihar) — a UNESCO World Heritage Site — was originally built by whom and rebuilt during which period?
options: Originally built by Ashoka (c. 250 BCE), rebuilt during the Gupta period (c. 4th-5th c. CE)
explanation: The Mahabodhi Temple at Bodh Gaya (Bihar) was originally built by Ashoka (c. 250 BCE, Mauryan period) — marking the site of the Buddha's enlightenment. It was rebuilt in its current form during the Gupta period (c. 4th-5th c. CE). It is one of the four most important Buddhist pilgrimage sites (Lumbini, Bodh Gaya, Sarnath, Kushinagar). UNESCO World Heritage Site (2002). Bihar's most iconic monument.

q: Which of the following is NOT one of the four main schools of ancient Indian art?
options: The Mughal School (Mughal art is medieval, not ancient — the four ancient schools are Mauryan, Gandhara, Mathura, and Amaravati)
explanation: The four main schools of ancient Indian art are: (1) Mauryan (polished stone pillars, animal capitals), (2) Gandhara (Greco-Roman, Kushana period), (3) Mathura (indigenous Indian, Kushana period), (4) Amaravati (Satavahana period, white marble). The Mughal School is medieval (16th-18th c. CE) — not ancient. Ancient Indian art spans from the Mauryan (c. 322 BCE) to the Gupta period (c. 550 CE).
